rationale.R1.1: The open-ended request invites assessment of the thesis, contribution framing, and claimed gap in the proposed solver narrative.
rationale.R1.2: Evaluating what one thinks of the draft centrally involves checking whether the theoretical and practical claims follow coherently from the stated premises.
rationale.R1.3: The artifact makes empirical performance and recommendation claims, so feedback would be expected to assess whether those claims are supported as reported.
rationale.R1.4: The text contains an explicit first-of-its-kind novelty claim, making novelty assessment a central responsibility.
rationale.R1.5: The delegation concerns the whole artifact, so section-level ordering and narrative progression are relevant to judging its effectiveness.
rationale.R1.6: A general reaction to a research-writing artifact naturally includes sentence- and paragraph-level clarity, register, and concision.
rationale.R1.7: The artifact relies on named prior work and theorem attribution, so citation coverage and accuracy are within the expected assessment.
rationale.RX.1: Any projected evaluation should disclose uncertainty because the artifact is only an excerpt and external facts are not being verified.
rationale.RX.2: The projection must avoid encouraging unsupported strength in claims such as novelty, convergence guarantees, and practical superiority.
rationale.RX.3: The response must remain limited to projecting responsibilities rather than actually critiquing or revising the artifact.
rationale.RX.4: The projection should account for possible downstream harm from overstated mathematical or empirical claims in research communication.
rationale.RX.5: The projection should preserve traceability to the delegation wording and the specific claims present in the artifact.
